executive_summary.one_paragraph: CD81 is a four-pass tetraspanin with a large extracellular loop (LEL) that is unambiguously surface-accessible across multiple independent human cell types. Live-cell flow cytometry with CRISPR-KO controls, surface biotinylation/MS with >2-fold enrichment, and cryo-EM/crystal structures resolving the accessible LEL collectively establish direct_multi_method evidence. Surface levels are constitutive on B cells and AML blasts, elevated on LSC-enriched subpopulations and under GBM hypoxia, and absent on HepG2 WT cells — conferring moderate state dependence. A significant intracellular endosomal pool exists in standard culture conditions but does not negate robust plasma-membrane presence.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: CD81 surface accessibility is supported by multiple independent direct methods from multiple independent sources. (1) Live-cell non-permeabilized flow cytometry on HEK293T cells (PMC13098096), AML primary bone marrow blasts (PMC13100155), and HepG2 cells (PMC11423668) — all with KO or WT negative controls confirming antibody specificity. (2) Surface biotinylation with membrane-impermeant Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin followed by MS (PMC12749419, >2-fold enrichment by ≥2 biotinylation strategies) and by WB (PMC10854524, PMC10614838). (3) Cryo-EM structural model resolving full-length CD81 topology with accessible extracellular loop (PMC10251933) and crystal structure of CD81-LEL (PMC8285954). No meaningful contradictions detected; one permeabilized IF (a1_evi_24) cannot discriminate surface from intracellular but does not contradict surface localization. The combination of live-cell flow cytometry and surface biotinylation/MS from independent groups meets direct_multi_method criteria.

antibodies.name: anti-CD81
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by PanKO (CD9/CD63/CD81 triple KO) and single CD81KO HEK293T cells showing complete absence of signal.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T WT cells positive for CD81 surface staining
HEK293T WT-derived extracellular vesicles positive for CD81 surface staining
HEK293T PanKO cells (CD9/CD63/CD81 triple KO) — no detectable CD81 staining
HEK293T CD81KO cells — no detectable CD81 staining
AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis — high CD81 surface expression; hematogones used as internal reference
Wild-type HepG2 cells — CD81 surface expression absent
HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 — high CD81 surface expression

antibodies.name: anti-CD19
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: CD81 surface trafficking indirectly confirmed via 3-fold increase in CD19-APEX2 surface staining upon CD81 co-transfection.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T cells co-transfected with CD19-APEX2 and CD81; CD81 co-expression increases CD19-APEX2 surface staining 3-fold, confirming CD81 promotes surface delivery

antibodies.name: anti-CD81
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Part of MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec); detects 37 EV surface epitopes simultaneously on intact vesicles using antibody-coated capture beads.

expression_observations.context: Small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) profiled with MACSPlex Exosome Kit for 37 surface epitopes including CD81
Glioma cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG) surface-biotinylated with membrane-impermeable EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (Thermo Scientific #21331); CD81 identified in surfaceome fraction
WI-38 fibroblast-derived medium EVs (mEVs) and small EVs (sEVs) surface-labeled with EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (ThermoFisher #21331, 10 mM); CD81 confirmed by WB after streptavidin capture

LC-MS/MS on surface-biotinylation-enriched fractions; CD81 identified with >2-fold enrichment and confirmed by at least two independent biotinylation strategies alongside CD9, ITGB1, GPM6A, MYADM, PROCR, PTGFRN, THY1

antibodies.name: anti-CD81
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used at 1:500 in 5% BSA for WB; no KO validation described in this source.

expression_observations.context: WB in EV fractions alongside CD63 and CD9; absence of calnexin (ER marker) confirms minimal organelle contamination; surface-vs-intracellular topology not directly resolved
HeLa EV surface protein preparation: CD81 detected by WB alongside GAPDH (intracellular loading control) in whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein fractions

antibodies.name: anti-CD81
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by siRNA silencing in ARPE-19 cells (Supplementary Figure 2). Details in Supplementary Table 2.

expression_observations.context: ARPE-19 cells fixed and stained for CD81 after EGF stimulation; permeabilization status ambiguous but staining context is post-fixation membrane-associated nanodomain imaging
HEK293T cells permeabilized with 0.1% Triton X-100 before primary antibody; CD81 IF co-stained with RAB5 (endosomal marker); detects total (surface + intracellular) CD81 pool

non_surface_expression.context: HEK293T cells, permeabilized IF with Triton X-100 detecting CD81 alongside RAB5 (endosomal marker); does not resolve surface vs. intracellular compartment
Unspecified EV fractions by WB (whole-fraction, no surface biotinylation), CD81 detected alongside CD63/CD9, absence of calnexin; topology not resolved

therapeutic_engagement.description: The ledger documents coltuximab (an anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody) resolved in a cryo-EM structure alongside full-length CD81 and CD19 in complex (a1_evi_26). Coltuximab is a clinically used anti-CD19 antibody; while it targets CD19 directly, CD81 is structurally resolved as an obligate surface partner in the same complex. No ledger claim documents a therapeutic that directly binds CD81 itself in a clinical or preclinical in vivo setting; the coltuximab structure is the closest therapeutic-proximate evidence present.
therapeutic_engagement.surface_form_rationale: CD81 is a four-pass transmembrane tetraspanin with no documented secreted isoform in this ledger. The large extracellular loop (LEL) is structurally resolved (crystal structure, a1_evi_25; cryo-EM, a1_evi_26) and is surface-accessible on intact cells confirmed by live-cell flow cytometry and surface biotinylation across multiple cell types. Only the membrane-anchored form is relevant; no secreted form is documented in this ledger.

cell_types.cell_type: breast cancer cells and normal breast epithelial cells

cell_states.descriptor: Hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome, with CD81 identified as a surface-accessible ADC target candidate specifically under hypoxic conditions in glioblastoma cells.
CD81 is enriched in leukemia stem cell (LSC)-containing subpopulations in AML, where it supports LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy, marking a therapy-resistant stem-like state.

CD81 is detected in extracellular vesicles from senescent WI-38 fibroblasts (replicative, irradiation-induced, and etoposide-induced senescence) as well as proliferating cells, present across both states.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal hematopoietic precursors (hematogones) in bone marrow
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: AML blasts at diagnosis, particularly non-CBF AML subtype
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 surface expression is significantly elevated on non-CBF AML blasts compared to normal hematogones; CBF AML retains lower CD81 surface levels similar to favorable-prognosis controls. Heterogeneous distribution (bimodal) across 290 AML patients.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: High surface CD81 on non-CBF AML blasts makes the epitope broadly accessible for antibody-based targeting; lower expression in CBF AML limits accessibility in that subtype. Subtype stratification is critical for therapeutic window.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Leukemia stem cell (LSC)-containing subpopulation within AML; therapy-resistant stem-like state
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 is enriched on LSC-containing subpopulations compared to bulk blasts, supporting LSC maintenance and chemotherapy resistance. This represents a cell-state-dependent upregulation within the leukemic hierarchy.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Elevated surface CD81 on the therapy-resistant LSC fraction enhances accessibility for targeting precisely the most clinically dangerous cell population, making CD81 a candidate for LSC-directed therapy.

accessibility_modulation.change: Hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome; CD81 is identified as a candidate ADC target that is surface-accessible specifically under hypoxic conditions, suggesting induction or redistribution to the surface upon hypoxia.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD81 surface exposure on GBM cells increases under hypoxia, the dominant microenvironment in solid tumors. This selective upregulation enhances epitope accessibility in the tumor core and supports CD81 as an ADC target in GBM.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: HEK293T cells under standard culture conditions
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 is distributed between the plasma membrane, perinuclear granular cytoplasmic compartments, and RAB5-positive early endosomes. A significant intracellular pool co-localizes with early endosomal marker RAB5.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Only the plasma membrane fraction of CD81 is accessible to extracellular binders. The endosomal pool is inaccessible without internalization. Binder efficacy will reflect the ratio of surface to endosomal CD81 at any given time.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Wild-type HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma cells (endogenous expression)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: HepG2 cells with exogenous CD81 overexpression (mimicking high-expression disease states)
accessibility_modulation.change: Endogenous CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells; surface CD81 only appears at high levels upon forced overexpression. This reveals that not all HCC-derived cells express surface CD81 constitutively.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Absence of surface CD81 on HepG2 WT cells means antibody or drug access is negligible in this hepatocellular carcinoma line; any therapeutic strategy must confirm surface expression in the specific tumor context before assuming accessibility.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Germ cells within seminiferous tubules of the testis
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 shows both cytoplasmic and membranous expression specifically in testicular germ cells within the seminiferous tubules, used as a tissue-specific positive control in IHC studies.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Membranous CD81 in testicular germ cells provides surface accessibility in this lineage. The tissue-restricted pattern means systemic binders may encounter on-target/off-tumor toxicity in reproductive tissue.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: B cells (normal and malignant), where CD81 functions as a BCR co-receptor alongside CD19 and CD22
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 is constitutively expressed on the surface of B cells as part of the BCR co-receptor complex. This lineage-specific surface expression is distinct from other cell types and is exploited in B-cell malignancy therapy.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD81 is a well-established, accessible surface target on B cells. Its constitutive surface presence in the hematopoietic B-cell lineage makes it a valid therapeutic target for B-cell malignancies with favorable epitope accessibility.

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: CD81 promotes surface delivery of CD19 (3-fold increase in CD19-APEX2 surface staining upon CD81 co-transfection), and itself reaches the surface constitutively. CD81 is not dependent on a partner for its own surface expression, but its chaperone role for CD19 means partner co-expression modulates the functional complex at the surface.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: CD81 constitutively co-clusters with EGFR in defined membrane nanodomains in ARPE-19 cells, and associates with tetraspanin-enriched microdomains broadly. This subdomain partitioning may limit epitope access for some binders but does not preclude surface engagement — the LEL is confirmed accessible by live-cell antibody staining.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: CD81 is a 4-TM tetraspanin with two extracellular loops: a small loop (SEL) and a large extracellular loop (LEL, ~80 residues). The LEL is structurally resolved by both crystal structure and cryo-EM, confirming a well-folded accessible domain of moderate size suitable for antibody engagement.

epitope_masking.rationale: The CD81 LEL is engaged by CD19 in the cryo-EM complex with coltuximab, indicating that portions of the LEL may be sterically occluded when CD81 is in the CD19/CD81 co-receptor complex. Tetraspanin LELs also adopt conformational states that can restrict accessible epitopes. Antibody binding to the CD19-bound face of CD81 LEL would be impaired in B cells where the complex is constitutive.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometric analysis of live HEK293T cells and their derived extracellular vesicles demonstrates surface expression of CD81, confirming plasma-membrane accessibility in an intact, non-permeabilized assay. CD81 was detected alongside CD9 and CD63 as canonical surface markers.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis showed that HEK293T WT cells and WT‐EVs were positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on their surface (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: Genetic KO validation: PanKO cells lacking CD9, CD63, and CD81 show complete absence of CD81 surface staining by flow cytometry, confirming antibody specificity for CD81 surface detection. This serves as knockout-validated negative control for the surface flow assay.

spans.quote: In contrast, PanKO cells (lacking the tetraspanins CD9, CD63, and CD81) showed no detectable staining for CD9, CD63, or CD81, confirming the knockout efficiency; this absence of tetraspanin expression was also observed in PanKO‐derived EVs, which lacked detectable levels of these markers (Figures 2B,C and S1D).

evidence.claim: Single CD81KO HEK293T cells and their derived EVs show complete absence of CD81 surface staining by flow cytometry, providing single-gene genetic KO validation of the surface detection method. This orthogonal confirmation upgrades validation_strategy to CRISPR_KO for the paired surface flow assay.

spans.quote: Further analysis of single tetraspanin KO confirmed absence of respective tetraspanin of the cells and derived EVs with CD9KO displaying negative staining of CD9, CD63KO cells and EVs were negative for CD63, and CD81KO cells and EVs were negative for CD81 (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: Methodological detail: APC-conjugated antibodies used for flow cytometry surface staining of tetraspanins including CD81 on intact cells and EVs, with single-cell KO models as validation controls. Surface staining performed on non-permeabilized samples.

spans.quote: To confirm the successful generation of single‐cell KO models for these tetraspanins, we stained both the cells and their corresponding EVs with APC‐conjugated antibodies.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry measurement of CD81 surface expression on AML bone marrow blast cells directly ex vivo, with hematogones as internal reference population. Representative histograms demonstrate surface accessibility of CD81 on primary human leukemic cells at diagnosis.

spans.quote: High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: CD81 cell surface abundance quantified by flow cytometry on primary AML blasts (n=290 total study cohort), with positive correlation to CD81 mRNA levels. Surface protein quantification by flow applied across two independent validation cohorts, establishing CD81 as a robustly surface-detectable protein on primary AML blasts.

spans.quote: In order to independently validate this finding, we applied the positive correlation found in our cohort between CD81 mRNA and cell surface abundance determined by flow cytometry (FCM) (Fig. 1c ) on two independent cohorts of AML.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry used to validate surface expression of CD81 on engineered HepG2 cells; method performed on live intact cells confirming plasma-membrane accessibility.

spans.quote: The resulting cells were validated by flow cytometry for surface expression of CD81 ( Figure 1a ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry surface expression assay on intact HepG2 cells shows CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 (negative control) and high on hCD81-overexpressing HepG2, demonstrating cell-surface accessibility of CD81 when expressed. OE construct stated as hCD81; signal peptide source unspecified — tier capped accordingly.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: Trafficking assay in HEK293T cells: CD81 co-transfection produces a 3-fold increase in CD19-APEX2 cell surface staining, confirming that CD81 promotes delivery of its binding partner CD19 to the cell surface. This functional assay indirectly confirms CD81 surface residence and its role as a chaperone for CD19 surface trafficking. Surface staining is on intact (non-permeabilized) cells.

spans.quote: Properly folded CD19 is trafficked to the cell surface by forming a complex with the tetraspanin CD81. 29 As an initial test for the functional integrity of our fusion construct, we used a trafficking assay in HEK293T cells to assess the ability of CD81 to enhance the delivery of CD19-APEX2 to the cell surface. 30 , 31 Our CD19-APEX2 protein matches the response of wild-type CD19, showing a 3-fold increase in cell surface staining when co-transfected with CD81 ( Figures S1B and S1C ), confirming that the fusion protein was properly transported to the cell surface and that its interaction with

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry surface-epitope profiling of small EVs using MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec), simultaneously detecting 37 surface epitopes including CD81. Kit uses antibody-coated capture beads for intact EV surface profiling without permeabilization, confirming surface accessibility of CD81 epitope on EVs.

spans.quote: Isolated sEVs were characterized by flow cytometry using the MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec, Bergisch Gladbach, Germany), which was developed for the simultaneous detection of 37 surface epitopes (CD1c, CD2, CD3, CD4, CD8, CD9, CD11c, CD14, CD19, CD20, CD24, CD25, CD29, CD31, CD40, CD41b, CD42a, CD44, CD45, CD49e, CD56, CD62P, CD63, CD69, CD81, CD86, CD105, CD133, CD142, CD146, CD209, CD326, HLA-ABC, HLA-DRDPDQ, MCSP, ROR1 and SSEA-4) that are known to be present on different exosomes and two isotype control beads.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation method using membrane-impermeable EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (Thermo Scientific #21331) at 1 mg/mL applied to intact cells at pH 8.0, with glycine quench, for surfaceome proteomics. Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin reagent is cell-impermeant and reacts only with extracellular amines, ensuring surface-restricted labeling.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: glioma cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG)

spans.quote: Cells were washed with PBS (with MgCl 2 and CaCl 2 Mg/Ca-PBS) at pH 8 prior to incubation with a 1 mg/mL solution of membrane-impermeable biotin (EZ-Link Sulfo-N-hydroxysuccinimide-SS-biotin, Thermo Scientific, 21331) for 30 min, rinsed, incubated with 0.1 M glycine for 10 min to stop the reaction, and rinsed again prior to processing of surface biotinylated proteins (surfaceome).

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation of extracellular vesicle surfaces using EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (ThermoFisher #21331) on pelleted mEV and sEV preparations; reagent vendor, catalog number, and concentration (10 mM) specified. This is a surface-restricted labeling protocol for EV surfaceome capture.

spans.quote: Following the first ultracentrifugation steps for mEVs and sEVs at 16,500 g and 100,000 g, respectively, pellets were resuspended in 10 mM EZ-Link™ Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (Cat No. 21331, ThermoFisher Scientific, Mississauga, ON, Canada) in PBS, pH 7.4.

evidence.claim: Western blot analysis after Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin surface labeling and streptavidin capture confirms CD81 presence as a known EV surface marker; CD81 detected alongside CD63 and CD9 as positive controls for EV surface protein enrichment. Method: WB on surface-biotinylated EV proteins.

spans.quote: WB analysis was used to confirm the presence of protein markers known to be present on Evs: CD63, CD9, and CD81 ( Figure 1 C).

evidence.claim: Mass spectrometry-based surfaceome analysis (LC-MS/MS on surface-biotinylation-enriched fractions) identifies CD81 among nine proteins enriched at the cell surface by proximity labeling and biotinylation. CD81 detected alongside canonical surface proteins CD9, ITGB1, THY1, and PTGFRN.

spans.quote: MS analysis identified GCPII as the most enriched protein, along with eight additional proteins previously detected in proximity labeling experiments: CD81 (CD81 antigen), CD9 (CD9 antigen), GPM6A (neuronal membrane glycoprotein M6-a), ITGB1 (integrin β1), MYADM (myeloid-associated differentiation marker), PROCR (endothelial protein C receptor), PTGFRN (prostaglandin F2 receptor negative regulator), and THY1 (Thy-1 membrane glycoprotein) (Fig. 3 B).

evidence.claim: CD81 shows >2-fold enrichment in surface biotinylation MS and identified by at least two independent biotinylation strategies, meeting stringent criteria for validated surface protein. Marked as high-confidence surface hit alongside CD9, ITGB1, GPM6A, MYADM, PROCR, PTGFRN, and THY1.

spans.quote: Eight proteins exhibiting > 2-fold enrichment and identified previously by at least two biotinylation strategies are marked in blue (CD81, CD9, GPM6A, ITGB1, MYADM, PROCR, PTGFRN, and THY1).

evidence.claim: Methodological detail for surface biotinylation + WB pipeline: streptavidin-bead enrichment of biotinylated surface proteins followed by Western blot analysis to confirm surface-captured proteins including CD81.

spans.quote: Following cell lysis and protein enrichment on streptavidin beads, we performed Western blot analysis of the resulting enriched proteins.

evidence.claim: Methodological detail for paired WB + streptavidin-bead enrichment step in surface biotinylation pipeline: protein eluants after streptavidin-bead enrichment analyzed by both Western blotting and mass spectrometry, anchoring the _check_wb_pairing requirement for the MS surfaceome result.

spans.quote: Protein eluants after streptavidin-bead enrichment were analyzed by Western blotting and mass spectrometry.

evidence.claim: CD81 used as EV surface marker loading control in Western blot validation of whole-cell lysate, total EV, and EV surface protein fractions; confirms CD81 surface accessibility in EV preparation context alongside GAPDH (intracellular control). Method: WB on surface-protein-enriched EV fractions.

spans.quote: We first validated the production of DPP4-Myc fusion protein, along with loading control GAPDH and EV marker CD81, in HeLa whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations.

evidence.claim: Antibody identifier for CD81 WB: Santa Cruz #SC-166029, dilution 1:500 in 5% BSA. Used for Western blot detection of CD81 in EV fractions. No explicit KO validation noted for this antibody in this source.

spans.quote: Membranes were then incubated with the following primary antibodies overnight at 4°C: CD63 (1:1000, EMD Millipore #CBL553), CD81 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐166029), CD9 (1:500, Cell Signaling #D801A), Calnexin (1:1000, Cell signaling #2433S), Annexin 1 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐12740), HSP90B1 (1:1000, Cell Signaling #7411), and Streptavidin HRP (1:10,000, Cell Signaling #3999) all diluted in 5% BSA.

evidence.claim: CD81 detected by WB in EV fractions alongside CD63 and CD9, with absence of ER marker calnexin confirming minimal organelle contamination. Whole-cell fractionation approach without surface biotinylation; CD81 used as EV marker but surface-vs-intracellular topology in this WB context is not directly resolved.

spans.quote: Western blot analysis revealed EV markers (CD81, CD63 and CD9) in EV fractions and the absence of the endoplasmic reticulum marker calnexin, suggesting minimal contamination from cellular organelles (Figure 1G ).

evidence.claim: Antibody identifier for CD81 IF/IHC: rabbit anti-CD81, 1:200 dilution, Abcam catalog ab219209. Used for immunofluorescence detection. Load-bearing for MethodObservation.antibodies[] slot.

spans.quote: Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-75459), mouse anti-CD63 (1/100, Abcam, ab8219), mouse anti-CD45-PE (1/25, BD Biosciences, 555483), rabbit anti-CD68 (1/800, Cell Signaling Technology, D4B9C), mouse anti-CD31 (1/100, Dako, JC70A), mouse anti-CD44 (1/100, Dako, DF1485), mouse anti-CD14-PE (1/25, BioLegend), rabbit anti-BCAN (1/200, Abcam, ab111719), rabbit anti-CD81 (1/200, Abcam, ab219209), rabbit anti-TF (kindly provided by Wolfram Ruf, Center for Thrombosis and Hemostasis, University of Mainz, Mainz, Germany), or rabbit anti-MCT2 (1/100, Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-112712).

evidence.claim: Methodological validation: CD81, CD82, and CD151 antibodies validated by siRNA silencing (shown in Supplementary Figure 2) for nanodomain immunofluorescence labeling experiments. Validation strategy = siRNA knockdown. Antibody details in Supplementary Table 2.

spans.quote: Primary antibodies used for nanodomain immunofluorescence labeling experiments are described in Supplementary Table 2 ; CD81, CD82 and CD151 antibodies were validated by siRNA silencing (Supplementary Figure 2 ).

evidence.claim: Immunofluorescence on fixed ARPE-19 cells using CD81 antibodies (cells first stimulated, then fixed and stained). Note: fixation without explicit permeabilization step described; if non-permeabilized, this is consistent with surface epitope accessibility; permeabilization status ambiguous from clip.

spans.quote: ARPE-19 cells stably expressing eGFP-clathrin were labeled with Fab-Cy3B (to label total EGFR) and stimulated with EGF as indicated, followed by fixation and staining with CD81 antibodies.

evidence.claim: CD81 immunofluorescence performed on permeabilized HEK293T cells (0.1% Triton X-100 permeabilization step before primary antibody), alongside RAB5 (endosomal marker). Because cells are permeabilized, this IF detects both surface and intracellular CD81; does not discriminate plasma membrane from intracellular compartment. Feeds non_surface_expression bucket.

spans.quote: For CD81 and RAB5 IF, HEK293T were seeded on optical coverslips and IF was similarly performed, with the addition of a 5 min permeabilization step (0.1% Triton X-100 in PBS) before primary Ab incubation overnight at 4 °C (0.1% FBS and 0.05% Triton X-100 in PBS).

evidence.claim: Crystal structure of human CD81 large extracellular domain (CD81-LEL) used for docking studies with HCV E2 peptide epitopes. Confirms the existence of a well-folded, structurally resolved large extracellular loop accessible for ligand engagement.

spans.quote: Because Ser 528 in epitope III is interchangeable with asparagine or threonine in the natural HCV variants, we tested Asn 528 - and Thr 528 -substituted peptides, assuming they take the same conformation displayed on the E2 structure, to see if they could dock onto the crystal structure of the human CD81 large extracellular domain (CD81-LEL) by using the Rosetta FastRelax protocols ( Fig. 5 ).

evidence.claim: Cryo-EM or structural model showing full-length CD81 (including complete ECD and TM helices) in complex with CD19 ECD/TM and the therapeutic antibody coltuximab (anti-CD19). Confirms 4-TM topology with extracellular large loop domain accessible for antibody engagement. Coltuximab (anti-CD19 mAb) contacts CD19 ECD while CD81 is resolved as a full-length structural partner.

spans.quote: The model shows the heavy and light chains of coltuximab (pink), the complete ECD and TM of CD19 (blue), and the full-length of CD81 (tan).

evidence.claim: UniProt annotation confirms CD81 is categorized as a membrane protein with at least one transmembrane domain (up to 12 TM domains across the dataset); CD81 identified in an MS surface-protein isolation study dataset among 136 membrane/surface proteins out of 456 total proteins detected. Supports multi-TM topology and surface localization classification.

spans.quote: Uniprot categorized 136 of the 456 proteins (29.82%) proteins as being present in membranes, on the cell membrane, or on the cell surface; 65 of them, including CD81, CD9, and CD63, have at least one (and up to 12) transmembrane domains ( Dataset S15 ).

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression is detectable by flow cytometry on AML blasts in bone marrow at diagnosis, and can be compared to hematogones (normal B-cell precursors) in the same sample. This establishes CD81 as a surface-accessible marker on leukemic cells in bone marrow tissue.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML blasts and hematogones, bone marrow

spans.quote: High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: CD81 cell surface expression is significantly lower on core-binding factor (CBF) AML blasts compared to non-CBF AML, establishing a disease-subtype-dependent modulation of CD81 surface levels in AML. CBF AML has favorable prognosis and lower CD81 surface expression.

cell_context.disease_state: acute myeloid leukemia (CBF vs non-CBF subtypes)

spans.quote: In this study, we show that core-binding factor (CBF) AML (n = 26), which have well-known favorable prognosis, 3 have lower CD81 cell surface expression compared to non-CBF AML (Fig. 1b , Supplementary Fig. 3a ).

evidence.claim: CBF-AML shows significantly lower CD81 mRNA expression compared to non-CBF AML, confirmed across two independent cohorts (Leucegene and Beat AML). This RNA-level difference is consistent with the observed surface protein differences between AML subtypes.

spans.quote: Consistent with our results, CBF-AML have significant lower CD81 mRNA expression compared to non-CBF AML shown for the Leucegene cohort 33 and the Beat AML dataset 34 (Fig. 1d ).

evidence.claim: High CD81 mRNA expression is associated with adverse overall survival in non-CBF AML and particularly in NPM1-mutant AML (HR=4.18 in NPM1-mutant subgroup). CD81 expression varies across AML molecular subtypes, with NPM1-mutant AML showing a trend toward lower CD81 expression.

spans.quote: Despite the exclusion of good prognosis CBF-AML and that CD81 expression was not different among other major AML subtypes (Supplementary Fig. 4 ), high diagnostic CD81 mRNA expression adversely affected overall survival (OS) in non-CBF AML and in NPM1- mutant AML using the Beat AML dataset (Fig. 1e ; non-CBF AML: HR [CI] = 1.74 [0.97–3.12]; NPM1 -mutant AML: HR [CI] = 4.18 [1.42–12.36]). 35 , 36 Of note, AML with NPM1 mutation showed lower CD81 expression, although this did not reach statistical significance

evidence.claim: CD81 is enriched within leukemia stem cell (LSC)-containing subpopulations in AML, where its expression supports LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy. This establishes CD81 as a cell-state marker that is upregulated in therapy-resistant, stem-like AML cells.

spans.quote: Notably, CD81 was enriched within LSC-containing subpopulations, where its expression supported LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy.

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression levels are heterogeneous across the AML patient population (n=290), with a bimodal distribution of low vs. high expressors. The violin plot summary and mRNA-protein correlation analysis confirm CD81 as a variable surface marker in AML.

spans.quote: The violin plot in the right panel summarizes low vs . high CD81 in the total AML study group (n = 290). b Violin plot comparing CD81 cell surface expression in CBF vs . non-CBF AML (n = 26, n = 226) and c scatterplot correlating CD81 z-score normalized mRNA vs .

evidence.claim: CD81 shows cytoplasmic and membranous expression in germ cells within the seminiferous tubules of the testis, used as a positive control tissue in an IHC study of retinoblastoma markers. This confirms CD81 protein expression at the plasma membrane and cytoplasm of testicular germ cells.

spans.quote: CD81: Cytoplasmic and membranous expression of the germ cells within the seminiferous tubules was considered as positive control ( Fig. 1 c1).

evidence.claim: CD81 protein is detectable by IHC in ovarian cancer tissues, where it is used as an exosomal surface marker in the context of cancer metastasis. This demonstrates CD81 expression in ovarian carcinoma tissue.

spans.quote: We have performed IHC analysis for exosomal surface marker CD81 in OvCa tissues as exosomes participate in preparing a premetastatic niche (PMN) for the migrating cancer cells at the secondary site and promote metastasis [ 32 ].

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression in glioblastoma (GBM) is profoundly reshaped by hypoxic stress; CD81 is identified as a potential ADC target candidate in GBM under hypoxic conditions. This establishes hypoxia as a modulator of CD81 surface accessibility in GBM cells.

spans.quote: We reveal how hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome, and validate several potential ADC target candidates; CXADR, CD47, CD81, BSG, and FXYD6.

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma cells but present at high levels upon CD81 overexpression. This indicates that HepG2 cells endogenously lack surface CD81, relevant for understanding hepatocyte surface accessibility in a tumor-derived cell line context.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: In HEK293T cells, CD81 shows a mixed subcellular distribution including perinuclear region, granular cytoplasmic compartments, and plasma membrane. This indicates that CD81 is not exclusively at the cell surface but also populates intracellular compartments in this cell line.

spans.quote: In this setting, chimeric proteins populated the perinuclear region, including a partial granular cytoplasmic and plasma membrane distribution matching the endogenous CD81 protein, in contrast to the whole cell body stained by GFP alone (Fig. 1 A and Fig.

evidence.claim: In HEK293T cells, CD81 shows cytoplasmic overlap with RAB5-positive early endosomes, indicating that a portion of CD81 localizes to early endosomal compartments rather than exclusively at the plasma membrane.

spans.quote: By counterstaining cells with an antibody recognizing RAB5, a marker of early endosomes [ 43 , 44 ], we found consistent cytoplasmic areas overlapping with GFP in confocal microscopy (Fig. 1 B).

evidence.claim: CD81 is significantly co-localized within EGFR-containing membrane objects in ARPE-19 epithelial cells under both basal and EGF-stimulated conditions, indicating constitutive co-clustering of CD81 with EGFR at the plasma membrane in retinal pigment epithelial cells.

spans.quote: In contrast, each of the tetraspanins CD81 (Fig. 2 ), CD82 (Supplementary Fig. 4A ) and CD151 (Supplementary Fig. 4B ) as well as clathrin (Fig. 2A , Supplementary Fig. 3 , 4 ) were significantly detected within EGFR objects in the basal and EGF-stimulated condition (Supplementary Fig. 3B–E ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry confirms CD81 surface expression on HEK293T wild-type cells and on extracellular vesicles derived from these cells, demonstrating accessible surface CD81 on both cell surface and EV membrane.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis showed that HEK293T WT cells and WT‐EVs were positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on their surface (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: CD81 is identified as a consistent EV surface marker across three breast cancer cell lines (MDA-MB-231, MCF7, MCF10A), detected in both small EVs (sEVs) and medium EVs (mEVs) by Western blot, indicating CD81 membrane expression in these breast cancer cell types.

cell_context.cell_type: breast cancer cells and normal breast epithelial cells

spans.quote: In sEVs and mEVs, 1315 and 1128 proteins were common to all cell lines ( Figure 2 C,D), and EV markers CD63, CD9, and CD81 probed by WB analysis were identified in sEVs and mEVs from each cell line.

evidence.claim: CD81 is present in extracellular vesicles from both proliferating (~PDL20) and senescent WI-38 fibroblasts (including replicative, irradiation-induced, and etoposide-induced senescence), as detected by LC-MS/MS proteomics. CD81 is expressed in normal human lung fibroblasts across proliferative and senescent cell states.

spans.quote: To elucidate the protein content of P-EVs and S-EVs, we performed liquid chromatography followed by tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) on whole EVs prepared from proliferating (~PDL20) WI-38 fibroblasts and from WI-38 fibroblasts rendered senescent by three different methods (S, IR, ETO).

evidence.claim: CD81 is expressed on B cells as a BCR co-receptor molecule alongside CD19 and CD22, making it a surface antigen on B cells targeted by engineered antibodies for B-cell malignancy therapy.

spans.quote: Consequently, engineered antibodies have been developed against other B cell antigens, including epitopes expressed by CD79b as part of the BCR, and the BCR co-receptor molecules, CD19, CD81 and CD22 ( Figure 1 ).

confidence_reasoning: A1 evidence_grade is direct_multi_method with zero contradicting entries. A2 confirms constitutive surface expression across B cells, AML blasts, glioma, breast, fibroblast, and retinal epithelial contexts with no dominant secreted or shed form. The intracellular endosomal pool (a2_evi_11, a2_evi_12) is acknowledged in surface_specificity=mixed but does not undermine the primary plasma-membrane localization. State dependence is moderate (absent on HepG2 WT; elevated in LSC and hypoxic GBM) but does not conflict with a high accessibility call in target-relevant contexts. Overall confidence is high.
